title: Reward magnitude determines reinforcement learning efficiency

data_scope.sample_sizes: Not stated in public abstract; see published article
data_scope.neural_recording: Ventral striatal dopamine (in vivo, exact method not available from abstract)
data_scope.causal_manipulation: Prolonged optogenetic enhancement of dopamine reward responses
data_scope.data_availability: Not stated in public metadata; full details in published article

core_conclusion: Outsized rewards dramatically accelerate reinforcement learning efficiency in mice across five behavioral paradigms through dissociable effects on within-session acquisition, across-session consolidation, and task engagement. Ventral striatal dopamine release duration and magnitude scale continuously with reward size; prolonged optogenetic boosting of dopamine partially reproduces the large-reward learning advantage. Animals' reinforcement learning capacity has been systematically underestimated, and absolute reward magnitude — not merely its presence — governs task engagement via dopamine signaling.
why_it_matters: Overturns the foundational assumption that small rewards maximize learning by enabling more trials; reveals dopamine as a quantitative learning-rate controller scaling with reward magnitude; dissects three mechanistically distinct channels (within-session, across-session, engagement); bridges biological and artificial RL by connecting absolute reward magnitude to learning efficiency.
why_nature: Paradigm-shifting claim with broad disciplinary reach challenging a century-old behavioral neuroscience assumption; provides complete causal chain from behavior to circuits to intervention (optogenetics); bridges behavioral ecology, neuropharmacology, and AI/RL algorithm design; implications for motivation disorders and rehabilitation.
added_value_to_my_research: Biologically calibrated large-reward curriculum for sample-efficient spatial RL in facility-location optimization
